coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted ultra-fast synthesis of carbon quantum dots from linter: Fluorescence cancer imaging and human cell growth inhibition properties
coredata.dc:creator: Eskalen, Hasan
Uruş, Serhan
Cömertpay, Sabahattin
Kurt, Akif Hakan
Özgan, Şükrü
coredata.dc:description: The water dispersed-fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) were synthesized using microwave-assisted hydrothermal process in the closed system from the waste cotton linter as a new carbon source. This method provided an ultra-fast, more effective, economical and easier synthesis for cancer-imaging applications compared to the other methods presented in literature. The morphological and optical properties of the hydrothermally produced carbon quantum dots were characterized by using FT-IR, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), SEM, EDX, UV–vis and fluorescent spectrophotometry techniques. An emission peak was observed at 420 nm when the CDs were excited at 376 nm. CDs were calculated to have an average particle diameter of 10.14 nm by TEM. In order to determine the usability of the CDs in cell imaging, two different concentrations (50 μL/mL and 100 μL/mL) of the CDs colloidal solution were applied to a human mesothelioma cell line, H2452, and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) for varying durations. While the fluorescent photos revealed that 2 h of exposure was sufficient for the cells to take the C-dots in and higher concentrations appeared brighter in the wavelengths studied (red channel (excitation/emission 586/646 nm), blue channel (390/446 nm) and the green channel (482/532 nm)), cell viability and proliferation assays indicated that the material was cytotoxic against the both cell lines and inhibited the cell growth in a time- and dose- dependent manner.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave
Fluorescence
Carbon dot
Linter
Cancer imaging
Cell inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The substance makes human cells visible under fluorescent light
C-dot slows down the growth and viability of H2452 mesothelioma cell line

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of carbon dots. (b) Size distribution of carbon dots.
UV–vis. and FT-IR spectra of carbon dot sample.
Carbon dot mixture in UV cabin at 365 nm.

Representative fluorescent pictures of the cells treated with 0, 0,6 and 1.2 μg/mL of carbon dots for 5 h. H2452 cells were counted and cultured as 5000 cells/per well in a 96 well plate. The next day, the cells were treated with 0, 0,6 and 1.2 μg/mL of carbon dots in triplicates. After 4 h of treatment, the medium were changed and the pictures were taken at two different wavelengths (446 nm: blue, 532 nm: green).

Representative pictures of the cells treated with 0, 0.6 and 1.2 μg/mL of fluorescent medium for 0, 24, 48 and 72 h. Right before the application of the compound, the pictures of the cells were taken and labeled as 0 h. Subsequently, the pictures were taken with 24 h intervals up to 72 h. While 48 and 72 h treatment appeared to influence the cell integrity in both concentrations, cells remained mostly unaffected by the presence of 0.6 μg/mL of the substance during the first 24 h.

Growth curves of the cells upon 0, 0,6 and 1,2 μg/mL of the carbon dot. The pictures taken for Fig. 9 were analyzed for the confluency of the cells using the software of JuliBr microscope. Their initial values were adjusted to 100 %, and all other values were calculated accordingly. Comparisons were made between the values at a given duration to the value at 0 h for each concentration separately. Only non-treated (0 μg/mL) sample significantly increased the cell number at all durations. **, **** represent cell viabilities significantly higher than the control at p values of <0.01 and <0.0001, respectively.

Cell viability is reduced by the application of the dot at studied concentrations. Non-treated cells (0 μg/mL) were adjusted as 100 %, all others were determined accordingly. Starting from 24 h, higher concentration of the dot reduced the viability while the lower concentration took 48 h to show suppressing effect. **, ***, **** represent cell viabilities significantly lower than the control at p values of <0.01, <0.001 and <0.0001, respectively.

Cell viability is reduced by the application of the dot at studied concentrations. Non-treated (0 μg/mL) cells were adjusted as 100 %, all others were determined accordingly. Starting from 24 h, all concentrations of carbon dots presented a suppressing effect. **, ***, **** represent cell viabilities significantly lower than the control at p values of <0.01, <0.001 and <0.0001, respectively.

Growth curves of the cells upon 0, 0,6 and 1,2 μg/mL of the carbon dot. The pictures taken for Fig. 12 were analyzed for the confluency of the cells using the software of JuliBr microscope. Their initial values were adjusted to 100 %, and all other values were calculated accordingly. Comparisons were made between the values at a given duration to the value at 0 h for each concentration separately. At the 24th and the 48th hours, only the higher concentration significantly (**p < 0.01) reduced the cell number compared to its control while at the 72nd hours, both concentrations were highly effective (****p < 0.0001). Non-treated cells increased the cell number in all durations.

Untreated samples from Figs. 10 and 13 were drawn together. The relative number of the cells in HUVEC cells (Fig. 13) were significantly higher than H2452 cell (H2452) foe each time point examined. * and ** represent statistically significance at the levels of p < 0.05 and 0.01, respectively.

Representative pictures of HUVEC cells treated with 0, 0.6 and 1.2 μg/mL of carbon dots containing medium for 0, 24, 48 and 72 h. Right before the application of the compound, the pictures of the cells were taken and labeled as 0 h. Subsequently, the pictures were taken with 24 h intervals up to 72 h. The cells appeared to be affected by the presence of the compound at all concentrations in all durations.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted ultra-fast synthesis of carbon quantum dots from linter: Fluorescence cancer imaging and human cell growth inhibition properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Art and Sciences, Kahramanmaraş Sütçü İmam University, 46100, Kahramanmaraş, 46100, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Art and Sciences, Kahramanmaraş Sütçü İmam University, 46100, Kahramanmaraş, 46100, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The water dispersed-fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) were synthesized using microwave-assisted hydrothermal process in the closed system from the waste cotton linter as a new carbon source. This method provided an ultra-fast, more effective, economical and easier synthesis for cancer-imaging applications compared to the other methods presented in literature. The morphological and optical properties of the hydrothermally produced carbon quantum dots were characterized by using FT-IR, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), SEM, EDX, UV–vis and fluorescent spectrophotometry techniques. An emission peak was observed at 420 nm when the CDs were excited at 376 nm. CDs were calculated to have an average particle diameter of 10.14 nm by TEM. In order to determine the usability of the CDs in cell imaging, two different concentrations (50 μL/mL and 100 μL/mL) of the CDs colloidal solution were applied to a human mesothelioma cell line, H2452, and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) for varying durations. While the fluorescent photos revealed that 2 h of exposure was sufficient for the cells to take the C-dots in and higher concentrations appeared brighter in the wavelengths studied (red channel (excitation/emission 586/646 nm), blue channel (390/446 nm) and the green channel (482/532 nm)), cell viability and proliferation assays indicated that the material was cytotoxic against the both cell lines and inhibited the cell growth in a time- and dose- dependent manner.

ce:section.ce:para: Cotton is one of the prominent agricultural products for not only the countries but also the world textile industry. Cotton production of Egypt approached to one million tons in 2011 (Hivechi et al., 2015). Turkey is ranked as the fifth in garment export in the world and the second in textile and garment supplier for EU (Çopur, 2017). Linter is; an important side products of cottons, obtained from their seed and they cannot be used in the textile process because of their short fibers (Morais et al., 2013; Tutuş et al., 2017). In 2010, the 42 million metric tons worldwide cotton produced and 2.5 million metric tons waste cotton linter generated (Abdullah-Al-Mamun et al., 2016). Obtaining carbon-based functionalised products from waste have been attracting attention of many researchers from different disciplines (Ma et al., 2017). The valuable products such as alpha-cellulose and carboxymethyl cellulose can be obtained from linter cottons that is the waste product from cotton seed production (Hivechi et al., 2015).

Since their accidently discovery in 2004, during purification of singled walled carbon nanotubes, carbon dots have been used fabrication of optoelectrical devices, chemical or biological sensing, imaging and photocataysis due to their low toxicity, good water solubility, tunable excitation and emission wavelength, and stable photoluminescence properties (Bagheri et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2018). Carbon dots are zero-dimensional spherical shaped carbon nanoparticles (Pang et al., 2017) which have been mainly produced by two different approach. At the top-down approach, carbon dots can be generated from the large carbon structure (Baker and Baker, 2010). Arc discharge methods, laser ablation methods and electrochemical synthesis are some examples of this approach. Combustion, hydrothermal/ solvothermal methods (Yang et al., 2019) and microwave irradiation are basic methods of bottom-up approach. The bottom-up approach is useful to produce large scale of carbon dots synthesis (Park et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2015). Among the bottom-up carbon dots production methods the microwave-assisted hydrothermal process is one of the prominent methods in terms of significantly reducing the reaction time. Moreover, this method also provide cost and energy efficiency (Angamuthu et al., 2018). For instance, in traditional hydrothermal method takes around to 24 h (Chen et al., 2017), nevertheless microwave-assisted hydrothermal methods to produce the carbon dots can generally be produced in 5−10 min (Angamuthu et al., 2018; Ðorđević et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2012).

Simple and inexpensive synthesis of carbon dots from different waste sources have been studied to produce the value added products (Adolfsson et al., 2015). In the literature, carbon dots could be produced from the different waste carbon sources including, orange waste (Prasannan and Imae, 2013), kitchen waste (Himaja et al., 2014), wheat straw (Yuan et al., 2015), whey (Devi et al., 2017), palm sell powder, food waste (Park et al., 2014), rice husk (Ngu et al., 2016), lemon peel waste (Tyagi et al., 2016), onion waste (Bandi et al., 2016), waste paper (Jeong et al., 2018), wine lees (Varisco et al., 2017) and data kernel (Amin et al., 2018). In this work, it was studied ultra-fast synthesis of carbon quantum dots from the cotton linter which was the waste of cotton seed production. The microwave-assisted hydrothermal method was used for the synthesized carbon dots. The obtained CDs were characterized by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Influence of the carbon dots produced from various sources on cell viability and proliferation have been also previously investigated and it has been realized that the most commonly used method for this purpose was MTS assay (Atchudan et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Vasimalai et al., 2018). In these studies, some C-dots were demonstrated to have cytotoxic effects on certain groups of cells (Liu et al., 2017; Vasimalai et al., 2018) while some were reported as ineffective for the others (Atchudan et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2017). When we performed similar experiments with a malignant mesothelioma cell line (H2452), we found out that our C-dots of cotton linter were cytotoxic to the cancer cells and were reducing their viability in a time- and dose-dependent manner.

Cotton waste linter was obtained from Set Tohumculuk Gıda Tarım San. ve Tic. A.Ş. local factory in Adana/Turkey. Solvents were analytical grade and purchased from standard sources. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The deionized water provided was used throughout all the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the carbon dots (CDs) were recorded by using Hitachi HT7700 with EXALENS, 120 kV, electron microscope. The surface morphology and EDX analysis of the nanocomposite ligands and the complexes were analyzed using Zeiss Evo LS10 SEM attached with Bruker Quantax EDS. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected on a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were performed by using Shimadzu-1800 UV–vis spectrometer. FT-IR spectra were obtained using KBr pellets with Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 400 system in the range 4000–400 cm−1.X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of samples was recorded by using a Philips X`Pert PRO XRD with Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.154056 nm, set at 40 kV and 30 mA). The microwave-assisted syntheses of CDs from linter were done in quartz vessels closed with teflon covers using Milestone StartSynth instrument with temperature control.

The ultra-fast and highly effective carbon quantum dots were synthesized from acidic linter waste using the closed microwave system in 5 min. 5 g acidic cotton linter waste was mixed with 20 mL water in a quatz tube and the tube was placed in teflon reactor, the Teflon lid was closed. The applied microwave program was given as below;
No t (min.) E (max. watt) T (0C)
1 1 400 150
2 4 400 150
The obtained product was filtrated with Whatman paper (Grade 309) and centrifuged at 15000 rpm. The pH of the filtrate was adjusted to 7.03 with NaOH and Na2CO3.

Malignant Mesothelioma (H2452) cells and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were purchased from ATCC (NY, USA) and cultured in 10 % (v/v) FBS (Gibco; Thermo Scientific, USA) and 1 % (v/v) pennicilin/streptomycin (Gibco; Thermo Scientific, USA) containing DMEM (Gibco; Thermo Scientific, USA) in an incubator (Panasonic, Gunma, Japan) at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 pressure.

In order to find out whether the CDs we produced had the potency to make the cells visible under fluorescent light, the cells were cultured at the density of 15.946 cells/cm2 per well in two 6 well plates. The next day, all wells were washed with PBS and the media supplemented with 0, 0,6 and 1,2 μg/mL of the product were applied to the cells as duplicates. Because of the fluorescent emission problem of the phenolic read in regular DMEM, the experimental solutions were prepared in DMEM without Phenol Red (Gibco; Thermo Scientific, USA). In one plate, fluorescent pictures were taken after 2 h of incubation whereas the cells in the other plate were incubated with the substance for 4 h before being visualized by EVOS FL Cell Imaging System (ThermoFisher). The cells were washed and kept in no-CD containing media during visualization.

Cells were counted and cultured in six well plates with the density of 16.640 cells/cm2. Upon attaching, the media of the cells were removed and the full media containing 0, 0,6 or 1,2 μg/mL of the substance were applied as triplicates. The pictures of the cells were taken by using Juli Br (South Korea, SK) at 0, 24, 48 and 72 h after the treatment. Four pictures of each well was used for analysis. Confluency of the cells was determined by the software of the microscope and the values were analyzed statistically via GraphPad Prism 6.0 (La Jolla, CA, USA).

The cells were plated on a 96 well plate so as to have 5 × 103 cells per well. In order to reveal the dose-time response, the cells were treated with two concentrations (0,6 or 1,2 μg/mL) of carbon dot for 24, 48 and 72 h. Cell viability was measured by MTS assay (CellTiter 96® AQueous Assay; Promega) using a spectrophotometer (Spektramax; BMG Labtech., Offenburg, Germany) as described by the manufacturer. The wells where no CD was applied were regarded as controls. Only media but no cell containing wells, on the other hand, were considered as blanks. Measurements were made for three independent experiments (n = 3) and three replicates were read for each condition. Absorbances at 490 nm were converted into viable cell percentage with the assumption that 100 % of the cells were alive in non-treated (control) group. Calculated values were analyzed via GraphPad Prism 6.0 (La Jolla, CA, USA).

The XRD pattern of the produced CDs from cotton linter gives a wide diffraction peak at about 2θ≈21°-28° with relatively low intensity as given in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peak of carbon dots indicated that the obtained particles are mostly amorphous nature (Ansi and Renuka, 2018; Chakraborty et al., 2018; Zhai et al., 2018).

Fig. 2 -a, shows the TEM image of water soluble carbon dots. Carbon dots generally dispersed as spherical in morphology can be seen from this figure. The diameter of the synthesized spherical-shaped carbon dots are given in Fig. 2-b. The diameter of CDs was ranging from within 1.8 nm–22 nm and the average particle size is calculated by counting 78 particles and it was found to be about 10.14 nm (Bac and GENÇ, 2017; Liu et al., 2017).

Carbon Dots having some substituents such as the aliphatic and aromatic groups are a kind of carbon allotropes formed of densely packed carbon atoms arranged as zero-dimensional spherical particles. According to the literatures (Murugan et al., 2019; Pourreza and Ghomi, 2019; Wu et al., 2017) our process is a chemical oxidation of acidic linter waste having H2SO4 under microvawe power like. The waste linter is obtained after the delintation of cotton seeds in sulfuric acid solution and it was used as received while synthesis of CDs. Thus, in the acidic oxidation process, cellulosic structure turned into carbon quantum dots having aromatic and aliphatic C–H, carboxylic (−COOH), alcoholic (−OH), etheric (-OR) and epoxidic groups. The detailed FTIR evolution of the used waste acidic linter, precipitated particles after microwave synthesis and obtained CDs were given in Fig. 3 . When evaluated FT-IR spectra of the product, it can be said that after microwave power in the closed system applied to the acidic linter, cellulosic bockbone was oxidized to some organic compunds having carboxylic acid, alcoholic, etheric and epoxidic groups. The wide absorption band at about 3300 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxylic groups of carboxylic and alcoholic structures. Aromatic and aliphatic CH stretches were seen at about 2900 cm−1 literatures (Murugan et al., 2019; Pourreza and Ghomi, 2019; Wu et al., 2017) Aliphatic groups can be formed over the carbonic structure, additionally to the aromatic structures forming the ring closing reactions. The carboxylate CO stretches were observed as a strong band at 1699 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum of CDs whereas not seen in FT-IR spectrum of linter (Fig. 3). Aromatic CC stretches also observed around 1600 cm−1 in CDs compared to linter. The very strong band at 1098 cm−1 from the etheric and epoxy COC stretchings removed in the CDs solution because of further oxidation glycosidic groups to carboxylic groups. Additionally, the small peaks between 1354−947 cm−1 were assigned to strectches of CO groups of acidic, alcoholic and epoxy structures (Fig. 3) (Ansi and Renuka, 2018; Zhai et al., 2018).

UV–Vis. absorption and photoluminescence spectra were investigated in order to evaluate optical properties of the carbon dot sample in water, n-π* transitions of CO groups from alcohol substituents was observed at 282 nm as a sharp absorption peak. π-π* absorption of CO groups from carboxilates was recorded at 222 nm with a small band (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The substance makes human cells visible under fluorescent light
ce:section.ce:para: As it can be seen in Fig. 8 , the pictures taken for the cells treated with 0.6 and 1.2 μg/mL of CDs for both 2 and 4 h, there was a clear increase in the fluorescent signaling at 446 nm wavelength (blue). 2 h was apparently enough for the cells to admit the compound in and it was clear that the visibility of the cells was time-dependent such that the signals produced by 4 -h-treated cells were stronger. When the pictures of the same cells were also taken at 532 nm wavelength (green), the fluorescent dye on the cells was still visible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dot slows down the growth and viability of H2452 mesothelioma cell line
ce:section.ce:para: Growth curve analysis suggested an anti-proliferative potential of C-dot against the cell line studied. Consequently, so as to reveal the cytotoxic influence of the compound on the mesothelioma cells, cells were plated in a 96 well plate and treated with 0, 0,6, 1,2 μg/mL of C-dot for 24, 48 and 72 h in four replicates. At the end of each time point, MTS reagent was added to the cell culture media and, after 4 h of incubation, absorbances at 490 nm were measured. For each condition, un-treated (0 μg/mL) cells were considered as 100 % viable and the viability of the cells in other conditions were calculated accordingly. The experiment was repeated in three times. Then, the values obtained through the calculation were plotted and analyzed using Graph Pad (Fig. 11 ).

The results of which our carbon-dots displayed an inhibition on the cells we examined might be considered from two aspects. In one hand, the material might be viewed as a potential substance which could be used to inhibit cancer cell growth. On the other hand, the fact that the cells to which the material was applied were deteriorated could be regarded as a sign of the general toxicity of the compound.

Although these first trials are providing us with an insight about how the CDs we produced might act on human cells, further investigations with more cell lines of various cancer types and the healthy cells of the same tissues (Farinha and Gascoyne, 2005; Tabak et al., 1999) are essential so as to understand the general toxicity of the compound.

However, when we evaluated the cancer-inhibition property of the compound and study the literature from this perspective, we encountered that anti-cancer features of carbon-dots have been frequently reported and these properties have been related to the protocol that has been used for the synthesis of the compound (Pierrat et al., 2015). In addition to the effect of methodology, the starter material was also claimed to influence the anti-cancerogenic potential of the final product (Hsu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2014).

Through the experiments we carried, we clearly showed that our CDs could be toxic to human cells regardless of their cancerous or non-transformed nature. It is also quite common to find the publications where the authors regarded their findings as an indication of the non-toxic feature of the compound, which increase the possibility of using the candidate molecule in various biomedical applications including live cell imaging. These claims are made mostly when the toxicity of the compound was low against the cells studied despite the fact that the cells of a cancer cell line was used for the investigation (Atchudan et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017). Our results emphasize that such general comments without evaluating the untransformed cells could be misleading.

In our last set of experiments, we wanted to investigate whether the higher toxicity we observed for HUVEC cells could be related to their growth rate. It has been long believed that cancer cells die more rapidly with chemotherapy because their proliferation rate is higher than normal cells (Mitchison, 2012). Since not all cells divide at the same rate in vitro, we checked whether the difference in CD cytotoxicity could be explained by the difference in the growth rates of the cells. As it can be seen in Fig. 14 , the growth rate of HUVEC cells were quite high.
When these two growth rates were drawn together, the difference for their relative cell number was statistically significant at each time point (Fig. 14).
How fast and efficiently CDs diminish HUVEC cells can also be seen in Fig. 15 .

Hasan Eskalen: Investigation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Serhan Uruş: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources. Sabahattin Cömertpay: Visualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Resources. Akif Hakan Kurt: Resources, Visualization. Şükrü Özgan: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Kahramanmaraş Sütçü İmam University (Project No: 2019/1-26 M, 2017/1-82 M, 2018/2-2YLS, 2018/2-18YLS, 2017/5-10YLS) for their financial supports. We are also grateful to Set Tohumculuk Gıda Tarım San. ve Tic. A.Ş. and Sercenk Kılıç (Production Manager).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indcrop.2020.112209.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abdullah-Al-Mamun et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A morphological study of microfibrillated cellulose prepared from waste cotton linter

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abdullah-Al-Mamun, M., Rahman, M., Khan, G.A., Uddin, M.H., Razzaque, S.A., Foisal, J., Hasanuzzaman, M., Rahman, S., Alam, M.S., 2016. A Morphological Study of Microfibrillated Cellulose Prepared from Waste Cotton Linter. American Chemical Science Journal 16, 1-8.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Adolfsson et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Valorization of cellulose and waste paper to graphene oxide quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Adolfsson, K.H., Hassanzadeh, S., Hakkarainen, M., 2015. Valorization of cellulose and waste paper to graphene oxide quantum dots. RSC Advances 5, 26550-26558.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Amin et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green and cost-effective synthesis of carbon dots from date kernel and their application as a novel switchable fluorescence probe for sensitive assay of Zoledronic acid drug in human serum and cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Amin, N., Afkhami, A., Hosseinzadeh, L., Madrakian, T., 2018. Green and cost-effective synthesis of carbon dots from date kernel and their application as a novel switchable fluorescence probe for sensitive assay of Zoledronic acid drug in human serum and cellular imaging. Analytica Chimica Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Angamuthu et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quick microwave assisted synthesis and in vitro imaging application of oxygen doped fluorescent carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Angamuthu, R., Rajendran, R., Vairamuthu, R., 2018. Quick Microwave Assisted Synthesis and In Vitro Imaging Application of Oxygen Doped Fluorescent Carbon Dots. Journal of Fluorescence 28, 959-966.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ansi and Renuka, 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Table sugar derived carbon dot–a naked eye sensor for toxic Pb2+ ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ansi, V., Renuka, N., 2018. Table sugar derived Carbon dot–a naked eye sensor for toxic Pb2+ ions. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 264, 67-75.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Atchudan et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for cell imaging using unripe fruit extract of Prunus mume

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Atchudan, R., Edison, T.N.J.I., Sethuraman, M.G., Lee, Y.R., 2016. Efficient synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for cell imaging using unripe fruit extract of Prunus mume. Applied Surface Science 384, 432-441.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bac and GENÇ, 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Naked eye and smartphone applicable detection of toxic mercury ions using fluorescent carbon nanodots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bac, B., GENÇ, R., 2017. Naked eye and smartphone applicable detection of toxic mercury ions using fluorescent carbon nanodots. Turkish Journal of Chemistry 41, 931-943.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bagheri et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New insight into the concept of carbonization degree in synthesis of carbon dots to achieve facile smartphone based sensing platform

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bagheri, Z., Ehtesabi, H., Rahmandoust, M., Ahadian, M.M., Hallaji, Z., Eskandari, F., Jokar, E., 2017. New insight into the concept of carbonization degree in synthesis of carbon dots to achieve facile smartphone based sensing platform. Scientific reports 7, 11013.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Baker and Baker, 2010
